cataloging_subject.label: Laȝamon’s Brut (Caligula version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the longer, more conservative Caligula version of Laȝamon’s Middle English verse history as represented by British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX and by the 16,096-line Brook-Leslie/U-M stream. It is not the abstract work embracing both versions, the substantially abridged and reworked Otho version, either physical codex, the 1847 or 1963-1978 editions, the OTA deposit, the U-M transformation, or the General Corpus XML artifact. The XML's 161 supplied 100-line groups and 418 printed-page milestones are navigation inherited from digital and edition layers, not authorial divisions.

catalog_summary.title: Laȝamon’s Brut (Caligula version)
catalog_summary.author: Laȝamon (also modernized as Layamon or Lawman)
catalog_summary.editor: G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie
catalog_summary.date: Probably later twelfth century, and before 1216 at the latest; exact composition date debated
catalog_summary.region: West Midlands, associated through the prologue with Areley Kings near Worcester; the Caligula witness was copied in the south-west Midlands

notes: The parenthetical version label is supplied for discovery and prevents the materially different Otho version from being collapsed into this record.

titles.value: Layamon's Brut (British Museum Ms. Cotton Caligula A.IX)
notes: The historical institution name and shelfmark punctuation are retained exactly as the U-M discovery title; the current holding institution is the British Library.
Goering identifies Brut as the modern conventional title, not a title proven to have been supplied by Laȝamon.
Cotton Caligula A IX f. 3r visibly opens with the red rubric ‘Incipit historia brutonum.’ The normalized title omits the incipit formula.
A manuscript-era title form reported in current scholarship; this review did not assign it to a specific surviving rubric or witness.

agents.name: Laȝamon
notes: The poem's prologue identifies Laȝamon as Leouenað's son and a priest at Ernleȝe, usually identified as Areley Kings. Biographical dates beyond activity around the turn of the thirteenth century are not secure.

agents.name: G. L. Brook
notes: Co-editor of the 1963-1978 EETS source edition; not a medieval work-level creator.

agents.name: R. F. Leslie
notes: Co-editor of the 1963-1978 EETS source edition; not a medieval work-level creator.

The self-identification in the prologue and current cataloging support the attribution; the exact historical process of composition remains partly recoverable only through the poem's own source account and later comparison.

The immediate and dominant literary relationship is to Wace's Roman de Brut. The prologue also claims use of English and Latin books, but their exact identities and passage-level contributions are not treated as established here.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship

notes: Brook's responsibility belongs to the edition layer linked in lineage.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship

notes: Leslie's responsibility belongs to the edition layer linked in lineage.

date_statements.display: Probably later twelfth century; before 1216 at the latest
notes: The repository header's single date 1205 is retained as legacy metadata but is not promoted to a securely established year. The normalized range follows the broader current scholarly statement.

date_statements.display: Second half of the thirteenth century (1250-1299), in Cotton MS Caligula A IX
notes: This is the date of the earliest surviving copy modeled for this version, not the composition date. Both surviving manuscripts are substantially later than composition.

places.label: Areley Kings
notes: The conventional identification of Laȝamon's Ernleȝe, north of Worcester.
This is a broad linguistic and cultural region; it does not prove a precise place of composition.
The prologue locates Laȝamon at Ernleȝe; the modern identification with Areley Kings is scholarly and catalog-based.
The narrative ranges beyond Britain but organizes a legendary history of the Britons from Trojan origins to the end of British rule.
This statement describes the production region of the Caligula witness, not automatically the work's place of composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: More specifically Early Middle English. Latin title forms and the modern English editorial layer do not make the medieval poem itself multilingual.

form_statements.description: A long verse history of slightly over 16,000 long lines. Long lines divide into half-lines and are linked variably by alliteration or rhyme; the Caligula manuscript's punctus elevatus signals the half-line division. The repository encoding represents 16,096 numbered L elements.

genre_statements.label: Historiography
notes: The poem reshapes legendary and historical traditions; the label does not imply modern critical historiography.

subject_statements.label: Legendary history of Britain and the Britons

scope.description: The extensive Arthurian reign, wars, and fall within the larger Brut history

summaries.value: Laȝamon recounts the legendary history of Britain from the aftermath of Troy and Brutus's foundation of the island through successive rulers, invasions, conversions, civil wars, Arthur's rise and fall, and the ending of British rule. Working chiefly from Wace's Roman de Brut while claiming other English and Latin books, the poet expands, localizes, and reshapes the Brut tradition in a long Early Middle English verse narrative whose long lines combine alliterative and rhyming techniques.

This record describes the Caligula version only. Cotton Caligula A IX is its principal and most complete witness. Cotton Otho C XIII transmits a later, abridged and reworked version and remains a related source rather than a coequal witness to every Caligula reading. Brook and Leslie's edition prints both streams; the OTA and U-M item selected the Caligula stream and omitted edition front matter, back matter, notes, and a facsimile.

extent.description: One counted verse work. The repository XML contains 16,096 unique consecutively numbered L elements, 161 DIV1 elements labeled as supplied line groups, 418 PB elements keyed to the source edition, and a final Amen in a CLOSER. The 16,096-line total and supplied 100-line groups are encoding/edition navigation, not a claim of authorial sectioning or universally canonical lineation.

content_parts.label: Laȝamon’s Brut, Caligula version
content_parts.summary: The complete Caligula-version stream represented by the repository encoding, from Laȝamon's prologue and Trojan origins through the close of British rule.
notes: No lower-level authorial contents structure is asserted. The XML's 161 supplied divisions are intentionally not represented as 161 intellectual parts.

related_works.title: Laȝamon’s Brut
notes: The abstract work has two materially different surviving versions, Caligula and Otho. This record fixes the boundary at Caligula.

related_works.title: Roman de Brut
notes: Wace's poem is the principal immediate literary source. This relation does not identify a particular physical exemplar used by Laȝamon.

evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the U-M identifier and title, Laȝamon attribution, legacy date, source-edition statement, Wace relationship, electronic omissions, editorial-symbol note, complete encoded text, supplied divisions, printed-page milestones, and final closing.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped structural inspection of LayCal.lineated.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Confirms 16,096 consecutive unique numbered lines, 161 explicitly supplied line-range divisions, 418 source-edition page milestones, and the final Amen represented outside the numbered sequence. It supports an encoding-specific extent while preventing navigation groups from being cataloged as authorial chapters.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, LayCal, Layamon's Brut (British Museum Ms. Cotton Caligula A.IX).
evidence.summary: Official item evidence for the title, author, Brook-Leslie publication statement, U-1682-C identity, public interface, supplied 100-line navigation, and item-level No Copyright-United States display.

evidence.citation: Nelson Goering, ‘Metrical Resolution in Early Middle English,’ in Prosody in Medieval English and Norse (British Academy / Oxford University Press, 2023), pp. 126-145, especially section 7.2.
evidence.summary: Current open scholarly synthesis supporting the modern-title boundary, two-version distinction, Caligula's conservative status, Otho's later abridgement/reworking, manuscript and composition chronology, Areley Kings and West Midlands association, slightly-over-16,000-line extent, and long-line alliterative/rhymed form.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Caligula A IX, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the current shelfmark, 1250-1299 date, south-west Midlands origin, parchment codex, physical dimensions, composite contents, Laȝamon and Areley Kings attribution, alliterative form, and the Brut's exact ff. 3r-194v extent as the most complete surviving copy.

evidence.citation: CiNii Books, Laȝamon, Brut, edited from British Museum MS Cotton Caligula A. IX and Cotton Otho C. XIII, NCID BA02706965.
evidence.summary: Bibliographic evidence for G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie, EETS Original Series 250 and 277, publication in 1963-1978, both named manuscript sources, and the volume split at lines 1-8020 and 8021-end.

evidence.citation: LayCal.lineated lineage manifest evidence for OTA deposit 1682 and its Caligula payload.
evidence.summary: Supports the two-file deposit boundary, Brook-Leslie source statement, Caligula/Otho stream distinction, encoding symbols, and omission of printed editorial matter.

evidence.citation: LayCal.lineated lineage manifest evidence and cache audit for the British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX IIIF object.
evidence.summary: Supports the complete 543-canvas codex coverage and exact mapping of the Brut at ff. 3r-194v to IIIF canvases 15-398.

open_questions.question: Can the composition date be narrowed, and what exact English and Latin works or exemplars underlie Laȝamon's prologue claim beyond the dominant use of Wace's Roman de Brut?
next_steps: Consult recent source studies and a current critical introduction.
Keep the broad 1150-1215 normalization and distinguish named works from hypothetical physical exemplars.
Map any additional source only at passages for which direct scholarship supports it.

open_questions.question: What is the exact Otho-witness title rubric and location associated in scholarship with the alternate form Libri Brutonum?
next_steps: Inspect Otho directly if a complete facsimile becomes available.
Consult codicological descriptions of the Otho manuscript.
Retain Libri Brutonum as an unassigned manuscript-era title form until a precise witness-level citation is secured.

open_questions.question: What large-scale divisions, if any, can be attributed to the medieval work rather than to Brook-Leslie lineation or U-M navigation?
next_steps: Review Brook and Leslie's introductions and the Caligula rubric sequence.
Do not convert the 161 supplied XML line groups into chapters.
Add stable intellectual parts only when manuscript or editorial evidence establishes their boundaries.

notes: The Caligula and Otho texts are versions of one abstract work but materially different textual streams; this record deliberately catalogs only Caligula.

The Caligula manuscript's copy date and region, the Brook-Leslie edition's publication dates, the 1992 OTA deposit date, and the 1993 U-M date describe different layers and are not merged into the work's composition date.
The 16,096 numbered lines are a practical locator inherited from the immediate edition and encoding, not a claim that the manuscript itself numbers or structures the poem this way.
Image availability, image-file reuse terms, modern-edition copyright, and digital-encoding rights are recorded by component in the lineage manifest; none is inferred from the medieval text's age.
